Financial materiality vs impact materiality
Financial Materiality refers to sustainability issues that significantly affect a company’s financial condition and operational performance. These issues—such as climate risks, social reputation, or executive bribery—can influence financial outcomes like cash flows, profitability, or asset values. For example, a company might face higher costs due to environmental regulations or supply chain disruptions from climate events. The focus here is on how these factors impact the company’s fundamentals and long-term value, providing critical information for investors to make informed decisions.
Impact Materiality, in contrast, focuses on the effects of a company’s activities on the economy, environment, and society. This includes aspects like greenhouse gas emissions, water usage, waste management, labor rights, or community impacts. For instance, a business might report on its efforts to reduce its carbon footprint or improve working conditions in its supply chain. The emphasis is on the company’s responsibility to external stakeholders and its contribution to sustainable development, highlighting its broader societal and environmental footprint.
Conducting a materiality assessment to identify material topics and subsequently reporting on a company’s performance, governance, strategy, and targets related to these topics forms the core of nearly all sustainability reporting. In practice, every type of standards mentions the interplay between a company and its external environment to some extent, though each has a distinct focus. Standards or frameworks emphasizing financial materiality—such as IIRC, SASB, ISSB, CDSB, TCFD, and TNFD—primarily target investors as their audience. Those focusing on impact materiality, like GRI, cater to a broader group of stakeholders, including regulators and communities. Meanwhile, ESRS and CDP balance both perspectives, prioritizing double materiality.
Framework vs standard
ESG frameworks provide the overarching structure and principles for sustainability reporting, guiding the “what” and “why”, for example, TNFD recommends that companies report biodiversity-related risks and opportunities across four dimensions: governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics and targets. While ESG standards provide detailed specifications and requirements, outlining the “how” and “to what extent”, for instance, GRI Standard 304-2 mandates that companies disclose the species they impact and the duration of those impacts, while SASB’s Metals & Mining industry standard requires companies to report the percentage of probable reserves located in sites with endangered species habitats.
ESG frameworks are typically adopted voluntarily by companies and serve as the foundation or inspiration for developing specific standards. For example, the TCFD framework has notably shaped the structure of the ISSB’s IFRS S2. In contrast, ESG standards can become mandatory when incorporated into regulatory regimes, such as the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which requires companies to disclose sustainability performance in accordance with the ESRS.
As the field of sustainability disclosure evolves, a broad range of stakeholders, including investors, increasingly demand a consistent and integrated global sustainability disclosure system. This will allow companies to reduce compliance burdens and generate comparable—even verifiable—sustainability reports across industries, which will enhance the quality of ESG raw data, making it more actionable for investors. As illustrated in the table above, various standards and frameworks are not in isolated competition. In fact, since the TCFD’s release in 2017, these disclosure systems have begun to reference, align with, and even merge with one another: In 2018, CDP redesigned its climate change questionnaire to align with the TCFD recommendations. In April of the same year, CDSB officially announced on its website that its reporting framework matched TCFD structure. In 2021, SASB and IIRC officially announced their merger to form the Value Reporting Foundation, and in 2022, they merged with CDSB into the IFRS Foundation. In 2023, TCFD announced its dissolution and transferred all its functions to ISSB. The IFRS S1 and S2 sustainability and climate disclosure standards released by ISSB in the same year fully adopted the 4 disclosure pillars and 11 recommended disclosures of TCFD and provided detailed guidance. Moreover, TNFD and the EU’s ESRS disclosure standard E1—Climate Change, released in 2023, have also adopted or integrated the TCFD framework.
Figure 2.2: TCFD Major Milestones 2016–2023
--

Asia’s sustainability disclosure landscape
In March 2025, PwC released The Sustainability Reporting Adoption Tracker, which monitors local regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements across 83 countries and regions spanning all continents via a questionnaire survey. This includes the ISSB standards, European Sustainability Reporting Standards, the SEC’s climate disclosure rules, and other region-specific obligations. For each jurisdiction, the Tracker summarizes their sustainability disclosure policy profile with high granularity, based on the following six questions: 
Which regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements is the territory adopting or has adopted? 
Is your territory going to mandate that entities report in accordance with the ISSB requirements?
When will the regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements be adopted?
Scope of companies included in the regulatory or legal sustainability reporting (e.g. assets/revenues greater than X)
Do the regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements apply to entities that are headquartered or listed in other territories?
What decisions have been made regarding who can provide required assurance opinion (i.e. financial statement auditor, other statutory auditor, and/or independent assurance service provider)?
The six questions mentioned above primarily target jurisdictions outside the EU, while the questionnaire for EU member states consists of four questions, structured similarly to the six questions but more focusing on ESRS, so we won't elaborate them in our main text.
It’s particularly noteworthy that this Adoption Tracker includes the implementation status of sustainability disclosure in 23 Asian countries/regions. I’ve condensed the questionnaire content into the table below. We can observe that in East Asia, China, Hong Kong SAR, Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan (China) have all either implemented or plan to implement mandatory sustainability disclosure requirements for local listed companies, with standards issued by local regulators fully aligned with the ISSB standards. A similar trend is evident in Southeast Asia, where Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand will adopt ISSB standards and phase in mandatory disclosure over time. Although Indonesia and the Philippines currently base their mandatory disclosure rules on GRI and local SEC guidelines respectively, Indonesia’s government released PSPK1&2 based on IFRS S1&S2 in December 2024, and the Philippines announced a roadmap for adopting IFRS S1&2 around the same time. In South Asia, India has implemented mandatory disclosure through the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) standard, which draws on GRI and SASB, with no plans to adopt ISSB standards. We can also note that nearly all jurisdictions prioritize large-cap companies for mandatory disclosure, employing a phased approach to expand the scope. 
Turning to West Asia, our earlier analysis of SSE data (see the table above) confirmed that Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Turkey have all issued sustainability disclosure guidelines, with at least a reference to GRI standards, encouraging companies to report ESG information based on existing standards. However, PwC’s findings indicate that only Bahrain, Jordan, Oman, the UAE, and Turkey have plans or requirements for mandatory disclosure. The first three prioritize GRI for disclosure, while the UAE’s mandatory requirements stem primarily from the Law on the Reduction of Climate Change Effects, set to take effect in May 2025. Only Turkey has fully adopted IFRS S1&S2. Meanwhile, Iraq, Israel, Lebanon, and Palestine have no plans for sustainability disclosure.

We can observe that countries in Asia vary significantly in both the progress and the standards of their sustainability disclosure requirements. This divergence raises several important questions for further discussion. For instance, will the ISSB standards eventually play a role similar to the IFRS accounting standards, serving as a “license to operate” in global capital markets? From the corporate perspective, how should firms weigh the trade-off between compliance costs and the benefits of market recognition? This dilemma may be especially pronounced for multinational corporations that must navigate and reconcile different ESG disclosure requirements across Asian jurisdictions.
Another dimension to consider is the expectations of investors. Do they primarily value “compliance-oriented disclosure”, which ensures comparability and regulatory alignment, or do they place greater emphasis on “strategic disclosure”, which reflects a firm’s unique sustainability vision and long-term competitiveness? At the regional level, one may also ask what explains the differences in regulatory approaches across Asia—whether they stem from varying levels of market development, political priorities, or institutional capacity.
This leads to a broader question: does Asia need its own region-specific ESG disclosure framework, a “regional version” of unified standards? If so, what would be the potential benefits, such as improved comparability and reduced compliance costs, and what challenges might arise, including political frictions, economic disparities, and sovereignty concerns?
Taken together, these perspectives highlight the importance of examining not only the future trajectory of ISSB adoption but also the best practices for corporate disclosure in a diverse and dynamic Asian context.

In 2018, CEO Larry Fink’s annual letter to corporate leaders marked a watershed moment, urging companies to articulate a “sense of purpose” and demonstrate their contributions to society as a prerequisite for long-term profitability. This argument signaled a departure from shareholder primacy and encouraged companies to consider the broader societal implications of their operations.
By 2020, BlackRock moved from rhetoric to strategy, incorporating ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) factors across all active portfolios, distancing itself from thermal coal, and positioning climate risk explicitly as investment risk. The surge in ESG-labelled funds and the firm’s increased support for climate- and social-related shareholder proposals cemented its role as a global leader in sustainable investing.
The landscape shifted between 2022 and 2023. BlackRock’s ESG funds experienced significant outflows, driven by underperformance and a surge of anti-ESG sentiment. In response, Larry Fink abandon the term “ESG,” emphasizing instead BlackRock’s fiduciary duty to clients and its role in providing comprehensive risk information, including climate-related risks. The firm maintained its stance that climate risk remains investment risk but sought to balance its approach by continuing investments in fossil fuel companies while encouraging energy transition strategies.  This nuanced position reflects the complexities of navigating stakeholder expectations, regulatory pressures, and market realities.
The evolution of BlackRock’s messaging continued in subsequent years. In the 2024 Chairman’s Letter, Fink replaced “ESG” with terms such as stakeholder capitalism and energy pragmatism, highlighting the need to balance the transition to renewable energy with concerns about energy security.  
By 2025, references to sustainability and net zero had all but disappeared from official communications, with the focus shifting toward democratizing access to private markets and infrastructure investments.
--

The late 1990s and early 2000s saw further progress. The 1997 Kyoto Protocol formalized international commitments to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, embedding environmental concerns within the ESG agenda. Meanwhile, corporate scandals such as Enron’s collapse highlighted the importance of governance and transparency. In 2004, the United Nations explicitly called for the integration of ESG factors into investment decisions, leading to the launch of the Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI) in 2006. This period marked the transition from ethical investing to a more systematic approach to ESG integration.
Major environmental disasters, such as the 2010 BP Deepwater Horizon oil spill, reinforced the need for measurable standards and accountability. The creation of the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB) in 2011 helped translate ESG issues into financial terms that investors could use. By 2015, ESG had become mainstream, with the adoption of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), the Paris Agreement, and the establishment of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD). 
In the 2020s, ESG considerations became central to financial regulation and corporate strategy across major economies. Disclosure frameworks such as GRI, TCFD, CSRD, and ISSB began shaping policy, and companies increasingly addressed issues beyond the environment, including labor standards and diversity. ESG matured into a multidimensional approach to value creation. These developments reflected a shift from shareholder primacy to stakeholder responsibility, embedding sustainability into the core of business legitimacy.
Recent backlash and controversy
Despite its rapid adoption, ESG has faced growing criticism and skepticism. Since 2023, the field has experienced policy reversals, capital outflows, and increased scrutiny. In the United States, ESG has become a politically charged topic, with some critics arguing that it prioritizes social agendas over financial returns and distorts market mechanisms. In Europe, the energy crisis following the Ukraine conflict reignited debates about the exclusion of traditional energy from ESG portfolios, with fiscal pressures leading to a more pragmatic approach to climate policy.
--
Why is ESG controversial?
The controversy surrounding ESG stems from several factors. First, the reason, perhaps, lies not in ideology but in finance itself. Early outperformance was often driven by favorable market trends rather than inherent advantages. By 2022, however, the surge in fossil fuel prices after the Ukraine conflict benefited sectors typically excluded from ESG portfolios, while technology stocks—often overweight in ESG funds—underperformed.
Policy debates have also played a role. In the US, some lawmakers argue that excluding fossil fuels threatens energy security and jobs, while in Europe, there is pushback against regulations seen as too restrictive. ESG-focused financial institutions and climate advocates have recently faced prominent antitrust allegations. Critics claim that these groups have coordinated efforts—such as shareholder activism and selective capital allocation—to pressure companies into reducing activities that generate high emissions. Some argue that such collective actions may limit competition or unfairly influence business decisions.
Additionally, ESG has sometimes become more of a marketing tool or compliance exercise than a genuine driver of long-term value. The complexity and cost of meeting diverse stakeholder expectations have led to overpromised targets and, in some cases, accusations of “greenwashing”.
Despite these challenges, it would be premature to declare the failure of sustainable finance. Impact investing in areas such as renewable energy, sustainable agriculture, and healthcare continues to grow. The current backlash may signal a period of adjustment, where the focus shifts from superficial compliance to more meaningful, long-term strategies for resilience and innovation.
The history of sustainable finance: timeline
The evolution of sustainable finance reflects a journey from early environmental awareness to a complex, multidimensional approach to value creation. While ESG principles have become central to global finance, recent backlash highlights unresolved tensions between financial returns, regulatory expectations, and broader societal goals. Maintaining an objective perspective is essential as the field continues to adapt and redefine its purpose.


--

What is next? 
In today’s climate change and shifting policies, organizations and governments face a crucial decision: should they scale back ESG initiatives or reaffirm their commitment to sustainability? This dilemma reflects a broader tension between immediate economic pressures and the long-term risks that sustainability frameworks are designed to address.
Climate change: a systemic risk
Despite ongoing debates about ESG, the reality is that climate risk is intensifying. For many, climate change may still seem distant or abstract, especially for those who have not directly experienced severe floods, heatwaves, or droughts. Historically, climate was viewed as a slow-moving backdrop to human activity, but the industrial era changed this dynamic. Human-driven emissions from fossil fuels have accelerated global warming at an unprecedented rate.
--

Source: Morningstar Direct, https://www.morningstar.com/business/insights/research/global-esg-flows.
For corporate leaders, the current environment calls for recalibration rather than retreat. Viewing ESG as merely a branding exercise or compliance burden misses its true purpose: to enhance resilience, innovation, and trust. Companies that align sustainability goals with measurable outcomes—such as energy efficiency or supply chain transparency—are already seeing improved performance and investor confidence. Regulators are also strengthening standards for sustainability reporting, pushing for greater transparency and consistency in financial markets.
The rise of artificial intelligence is further shaping the field. AI tools improve data quality, reduce reporting costs, and help link ESG practices to financial outcomes. They also enable real-time monitoring of ESG risks and support regulators in detecting greenwashing. However, the adoption of AI brings new challenges, such as concerns about job displacement, security, and governance. Ensuring that technology serves broader sustainable development goals will be essential.
Taken together, these risks and trends reaffirm the importance of sustainable finance. It is not just about balancing financial returns with ethical values; it is a necessary mechanism for closing the global financing gap and directing resources toward a low-carbon, equitable economy. As risks evolve, refining sustainable finance practices and understanding their impacts will be crucial for building resilience and supporting long-term development.
The ESG ecosystem
To understand how ESG principles are applied in practice, it is important to look at the main sectors and organizations involved in the ESG landscape. 
Global foundations and scientific leadership 
The United Nations has played a central role in shaping the scientific and policy foundations of environmental and climate action. Since its establishment in 1972, the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) has brought together governments, civil society, and the private sector to address major environmental challenges. In 1988, UNEP and the World Meteorological Organization created the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which provides regular scientific assessments on climate change. The IPCC’s reports are widely recognized as the most comprehensive sources of climate science and have directly influenced international policy, including the annual Conferences of the Parties (COPs) under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). These meetings, such as COP30 in Brazil, are where governments review progress toward global climate goals set in the Paris Agreement.
Industry initiatives and investor collaboration
Beyond global policy, industry-led initiatives have become important drivers of ESG practice. The Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI), launched in 2006, encourages investors to integrate ESG factors into their decisions. With over 5,000 signatories representing $139.6 trillion in assets under management, PRI demonstrates the scale of investor commitment. Other collaborative efforts include Climate Action 100+, which brings together more than 600 investors to engage with climate actions, and the Net Zero Asset Owner Alliance (NZAOA), whose members set clear targets for reducing portfolio emissions. These alliances show how financial institutions are working together to address climate risks and promote sustainability.
--
Investors, asset managers, and financial products
Investors are at the heart of the ESG ecosystem, using sustainability information to guide investment and risk management. Asset owners include pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, insurance companies, endowments, family offices, and retail investors. Asset managers oversee funds for these investors, including mutual funds, hedge funds, private equity, and infrastructure funds. On the financing side, banks and issuers structure ESG products such as green bonds and assess ESG risks when issuing debt.
As ESG has become more prominent, concerns about greenwashing and inconsistent practices have grown. Regulators and supervisors now play a key role in shaping the ESG landscape. International bodies like the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS), the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS), the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO), and the Financial Stability Board (FSB) have recognized sustainable risk alongside traditional financial risks. Governments are also active: the European Union leads with comprehensive ESG regulations, the UK has integrated ESG into corporate governance, and exchanges like the Hong Kong Stock Exchange require ESG reporting for listed companies.

Purpose and structure of this book
This chapter offers a global overview of sustainable finance, introducing its core concepts, historical development, key players, and regulatory frameworks. We also address the limitations and controversies that have emerged, especially in real-world applications.
The following chapters build on this foundation:
Chapter 2 examines the effects and drivers of corporate sustainable governance, exploring how companies implement and benefit from sustainability practices.
--
Corporate Finance and Sustainability 
Measure ESG
Generally speaking, there are four main components in sustainability (ESG) ecosystem. As we can see in Figure 2.1, Companies are positioned at the upstream end of the ESG data flow, serving as the primary source of most sustainability information that investors focus on—such as carbon emissions, employee diversity, and anti-bribery measures. They release this information either voluntarily or to meet mandatory regulatory demands in their regions, following established sustainability standards or frameworks, thus producing ESG raw data. The raw data is collected by data aggregators like Bloomberg and LSEG, which calculate commonly used ESG metrics (e.g., carbon emission intensity or green revenue percentage), and display them consistently on their platforms. Meanwhile, ESG rating agencies like MSCI and Sustainalytics also leverage this raw data alongside additional sources (e.g., news releases and expert interviews). Using their own rating methodologies, these agencies conduct thorough, systematic assessments of companies’ sustainability performance across sectors, providing standardized ratings to enable cross-company comparisons. The end users of the ESG data flow are banks or investors, who integrate these ESG metrics or ratings into their investment or risk management processes. For example, they might adjust loan interest rates based on a company’s ESG rating or use the carbon intensity of invested companies to shape engagement strategies or divestment decisions. 
According to the above illustration, it is clear that calculating sustainability metrics from ESG raw data and directly referencing ESG ratings represent unstructured and structured approaches, respectively, to evaluating a company’s ESG performance. The first part of this section will closely examine the leading sustainability disclosure standards or frameworks in use today, offering a clear view of how companies report their ESG raw data and highlighting the types of sustainability information that regulators, investors, and other stakeholders prioritize. The second part will focus on introducing key ESG rating agencies and explaining the core methodologies analysts employ to rate companies.

Financial materiality vs impact materiality
Financial Materiality refers to sustainability issues that significantly affect a company’s financial condition and operational performance. These issues—such as climate risks, social reputation, or executive bribery—can influence financial outcomes like cash flows, profitability, or asset values. For example, a company might face higher costs due to environmental regulations or supply chain disruptions from climate events. The focus here is on how these factors impact the company’s fundamentals and long-term value, providing critical information for investors to make informed decisions.
Impact Materiality, in contrast, focuses on the effects of a company’s activities on the economy, environment, and society. This includes aspects like greenhouse gas emissions, water usage, waste management, labor rights, or community impacts. For instance, a business might report on its efforts to reduce its carbon footprint or improve working conditions in its supply chain. The emphasis is on the company’s responsibility to external stakeholders and its contribution to sustainable development, highlighting its broader societal and environmental footprint.
Conducting a materiality assessment to identify material topics and subsequently reporting on a company’s performance, governance, strategy, and targets related to these topics forms the core of nearly all sustainability reporting. In practice, every type of standards mentions the interplay between a company and its external environment to some extent, though each has a distinct focus. Standards or frameworks emphasizing financial materiality—such as IIRC, SASB, ISSB, CDSB, TCFD, and TNFD—primarily target investors as their audience. Those focusing on impact materiality, like GRI, cater to a broader group of stakeholders, including regulators and communities. Meanwhile, ESRS and CDP balance both perspectives, prioritizing double materiality.
Framework vs standard
ESG frameworks provide the overarching structure and principles for sustainability reporting, guiding the “what” and “why”, for example, TNFD recommends that companies report biodiversity-related risks and opportunities across four dimensions: governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics and targets. While ESG standards provide detailed specifications and requirements, outlining the “how” and “to what extent”, for instance, GRI Standard 304-2 mandates that companies disclose the species they impact and the duration of those impacts, while SASB’s Metals & Mining industry standard requires companies to report the percentage of probable reserves located in sites with endangered species habitats.
ESG frameworks are typically adopted voluntarily by companies and serve as the foundation or inspiration for developing specific standards. For example, the TCFD framework has notably shaped the structure of the ISSB’s IFRS S2. In contrast, ESG standards can become mandatory when incorporated into regulatory regimes, such as the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which requires companies to disclose sustainability performance in accordance with the ESRS.
As the field of sustainability disclosure evolves, a broad range of stakeholders, including investors, increasingly demand a consistent and integrated global sustainability disclosure system. This will allow companies to reduce compliance burdens and generate comparable—even verifiable—sustainability reports across industries, which will enhance the quality of ESG raw data, making it more actionable for investors. As illustrated in the table above, various standards and frameworks are not in isolated competition. In fact, since the TCFD’s release in 2017, these disclosure systems have begun to reference, align with, and even merge with one another: In 2018, CDP redesigned its climate change questionnaire to align with the TCFD recommendations. In April of the same year, CDSB officially announced on its website that its reporting framework matched TCFD structure. In 2021, SASB and IIRC officially announced their merger to form the Value Reporting Foundation, and in 2022, they merged with CDSB into the IFRS Foundation. In 2023, TCFD announced its dissolution and transferred all its functions to ISSB. The IFRS S1 and S2 sustainability and climate disclosure standards released by ISSB in the same year fully adopted the 4 disclosure pillars and 11 recommended disclosures of TCFD and provided detailed guidance. Moreover, TNFD and the EU’s ESRS disclosure standard E1—Climate Change, released in 2023, have also adopted or integrated the TCFD framework.
Figure 2.2: TCFD Major Milestones 2016–2023
--

Asia’s sustainability disclosure landscape
In March 2025, PwC released The Sustainability Reporting Adoption Tracker, which monitors local regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements across 83 countries and regions spanning all continents via a questionnaire survey. This includes the ISSB standards, European Sustainability Reporting Standards, the SEC’s climate disclosure rules, and other region-specific obligations. For each jurisdiction, the Tracker summarizes their sustainability disclosure policy profile with high granularity, based on the following six questions: 
Which regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements is the territory adopting or has adopted? 
Is your territory going to mandate that entities report in accordance with the ISSB requirements?
When will the regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements be adopted?
Scope of companies included in the regulatory or legal sustainability reporting (e.g. assets/revenues greater than X)
Do the regulatory or legal sustainability reporting requirements apply to entities that are headquartered or listed in other territories?
What decisions have been made regarding who can provide required assurance opinion (i.e. financial statement auditor, other statutory auditor, and/or independent assurance service provider)?
The six questions mentioned above primarily target jurisdictions outside the EU, while the questionnaire for EU member states consists of four questions, structured similarly to the six questions but more focusing on ESRS, so we won't elaborate them in our main text.
It’s particularly noteworthy that this Adoption Tracker includes the implementation status of sustainability disclosure in 23 Asian countries/regions. I’ve condensed the questionnaire content into the table below. We can observe that in East Asia, China, Hong Kong SAR, Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan (China) have all either implemented or plan to implement mandatory sustainability disclosure requirements for local listed companies, with standards issued by local regulators fully aligned with the ISSB standards. A similar trend is evident in Southeast Asia, where Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand will adopt ISSB standards and phase in mandatory disclosure over time. Although Indonesia and the Philippines currently base their mandatory disclosure rules on GRI and local SEC guidelines respectively, Indonesia’s government released PSPK1&2 based on IFRS S1&S2 in December 2024, and the Philippines announced a roadmap for adopting IFRS S1&2 around the same time. In South Asia, India has implemented mandatory disclosure through the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) standard, which draws on GRI and SASB, with no plans to adopt ISSB standards. We can also note that nearly all jurisdictions prioritize large-cap companies for mandatory disclosure, employing a phased approach to expand the scope. 
Turning to West Asia, our earlier analysis of SSE data (see the table above) confirmed that Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Turkey have all issued sustainability disclosure guidelines, with at least a reference to GRI standards, encouraging companies to report ESG information based on existing standards. However, PwC’s findings indicate that only Bahrain, Jordan, Oman, the UAE, and Turkey have plans or requirements for mandatory disclosure. The first three prioritize GRI for disclosure, while the UAE’s mandatory requirements stem primarily from the Law on the Reduction of Climate Change Effects, set to take effect in May 2025. Only Turkey has fully adopted IFRS S1&S2. Meanwhile, Iraq, Israel, Lebanon, and Palestine have no plans for sustainability disclosure.

We can observe that countries in Asia vary significantly in both the progress and the standards of their sustainability disclosure requirements. This divergence raises several important questions for further discussion. For instance, will the ISSB standards eventually play a role similar to the IFRS accounting standards, serving as a “license to operate” in global capital markets? From the corporate perspective, how should firms weigh the trade-off between compliance costs and the benefits of market recognition? This dilemma may be especially pronounced for multinational corporations that must navigate and reconcile different ESG disclosure requirements across Asian jurisdictions.
Another dimension to consider is the expectations of investors. Do they primarily value “compliance-oriented disclosure”, which ensures comparability and regulatory alignment, or do they place greater emphasis on “strategic disclosure”, which reflects a firm’s unique sustainability vision and long-term competitiveness? At the regional level, one may also ask what explains the differences in regulatory approaches across Asia—whether they stem from varying levels of market development, political priorities, or institutional capacity.
This leads to a broader question: does Asia need its own region-specific ESG disclosure framework, a “regional version” of unified standards? If so, what would be the potential benefits, such as improved comparability and reduced compliance costs, and what challenges might arise, including political frictions, economic disparities, and sovereignty concerns?
Taken together, these perspectives highlight the importance of examining not only the future trajectory of ISSB adoption but also the best practices for corporate disclosure in a diverse and dynamic Asian context.

The challenges of sustainable finance extend beyond climate. Social issues—including labor rights, gender equality, education, healthcare, and ethical supply chains—remain unresolved, emphasizing that investment in people is central to sustainable progress. The global financing gap for sustainability remains significant, especially in developing countries. Bridging this gap requires effective integration of public and private capital, as well as financial instruments that balance short-term costs with long-term benefits.
Taken together, these risks and trends reaffirm the importance of sustainable finance. It is not just about balancing financial returns with ethical values; it is a necessary mechanism for closing the global financing gap and directing resources toward a low-carbon, equitable economy. As risks evolve, refining sustainable finance practices and understanding their impacts will be crucial for building resilience and supporting long-term development.
Financial Materiality refers to sustainability issues that significantly affect a company’s financial condition and operational performance. These issues—such as climate risks, social reputation, or executive bribery—can influence financial outcomes like cash flows, profitability, or asset values. For example, a company might face higher costs due to environmental regulations or supply chain disruptions from climate events. The focus here is on how these factors impact the company’s fundamentals and long-term value, providing critical information for investors to make informed decisions.
We can observe that countries in Asia vary significantly in both the progress and the standards of their sustainability disclosure requirements. This divergence raises several important questions for further discussion. For instance, will the ISSB standards eventually play a role similar to the IFRS accounting standards, serving as a “license to operate” in global capital markets? From the corporate perspective, how should firms weigh the trade-off between compliance costs and the benefits of market recognition? This dilemma may be especially pronounced for multinational corporations that must navigate and reconcile different ESG disclosure requirements across Asian jurisdictions.
In addition to issuing bonds in public markets or securing bank loans, companies can raise funds by selling equity. This can be done by attracting venture capital or private equity investments, or, for companies eligible to go public, through an initial public offering (IPO). And companies can further raise funds through secondary stock offerings. Consequently, the cost of equity financing is a critical factor for companies to consider. Academic research estimates the cost of equity financing (i.e., investors’ expected returns) using two main approaches: the Implied Cost of Capital (ICC) and factor models based on realized returns (e.g., CAPM). The ICC is an ex-ante measure of equity cost, calculated as the internal rate of return that equates the current stock price with the present value of expected future cash flows. In contrast, factor models like CAPM are ex-post methods, relying on historical return data from market indices, government bonds, and individual stocks to estimate the cost of equity. Similar to the mechanisms by which ESG impacts the cost of debt financing, a company’s sustainability practices can theoretically create long-term value for shareholders by reducing information asymmetry, mitigating various risks in business operations, and aligning with customers’ sustainable purchasing preferences, thereby lowering the cost of equity financing. In fact, a substantial body of empirical research has tested these influence mechanisms and their effects.
In the preceding sections, we have summarized academic research on how corporate sustainability performance affects the cost of debt and equity financing, as well as the mechanisms behind these effects. Moreover, some studies have explored the impact of sustainability on a company’s overall cost of capital, typically measured by the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC), which reflects the average cost of debt and equity financing, weighted by their proportions in the capital structure. Most empirical studies confirm that companies with strong sustainability practices are rewarded with a lower WACC, and we won’t reiterate those findings here. However, it’s worth noting that some studies have produced inconsistent results. For instance, Tron et al. (2025) conducted regression analyses by grouping global listed companies by industry and found that improvements in sustainability performance did not significantly impact WACC across industries. Besides, Gjergji et al. (2021)’s study on small and medium-sized Italian listed companies revealed that sustainability disclosures actually increased WACC. The authors offered three explanations: 1) SMEs typically have simpler management structures with less information asymmetry between managers and owners, and banks, as the primary financing source, provide significant oversight, diminishing the role of ESG disclosure in reducing information asymmetry; 2) ESG disclosure requires technical expertise, advertising skills, organizational planning, and ongoing management, which are costly, and SMEs, with limited resources, may see their profitability squeezed by such investments; 3) SMEs often focus on niche markets, and disclosing ESG information could lead to the leakage of sensitive proprietary information, increasing the risk of imitation by competitors and threatening their competitive advantage. These findings offer a new perspective on tailoring sustainability disclosure requirements for companies of different sizes and types.
A company’s sustainability performance not only affects its cost of capital but also influences its choice of financing methods, thereby shaping its capital structure. According to the pecking order theory(Myers and Majluf, 1984), differences in information asymmetry between different kinds of capital providers and the company, as well as varying sensitivities to the same information, lead to disparities in financing costs. As a result, companies follow a specific hierarchy when selecting financing methods to minimize costs. Specifically, companies prioritize using retained earnings. If internal funds are insufficient, they prefer debt financing, as creditors are less sensitive to the company’s information(Höck et al., 2023). This lower sensitivity means creditors focus primarily on the company’s ability to repay, without interpreting borrowing as a sign of operational issues that would prompt higher interest rates. Moreover, debt financing offers tax shield benefits. In contrast, equity financing (issuing new shares) is often perceived by external investors as a signal that the company’s value is overinflated or that it may be facing operational challenges, potentially leading to a stock price decline and higher financing costs. In other words, companies seek more internal financing sources (Denis and Mihov, 2003).
Reduced myopic actions: Greater transparency in ESG disclosures narrows the information gap between corporate managers, shareholders, and other stakeholders. This discourages managerial rent-seeking or short-term opportunistic behaviors, such as aggressive earnings management, insider trading, or related-party transactions. Moreover, sustainability policies encourage management to adopt a long-term perspective, ultimately enhancing firm value over time.
Alignment with Stakeholder Interests: ESG practices can mitigate information asymmetry between corporate managers and various stakeholders, fostering stronger relationships with them. By doing so, these practices help balance and address the diverse demands of stakeholders, reducing agency costs and ultimately promoting the long-term value of the firm. From a consumer perspective, companies with strong ESG propositions tend to build customer loyalty and attract new market segments. From a partner perspective, large suppliers increasingly integrate ESG factors into their value chains, prioritizing sustainability in water use, packaging, and plastics. These suppliers prefer to collaborate with businesses that share their values, while resource-efficient firms can also reduce per-unit production costs. From an employee perspective, younger workers and millennials seek meaningful work, and companies with sustainability commitments are better positioned to attract and retain talent, often benefiting from higher productivity and efficiency. From a regulatory perspective, policymakers rely on the corporate sector to address issues like environmental pollution and workplace diversity, while, sustainable practices like a smaller environmental footprint can also reduce the likelihood of regulatory penalties. Finally, from an investor perspective, corporates’ ESG-focused projects—such as developing renewable energy plants—help avoid stranded assets (e.g. coal-fired power plants), mitigate downside risks, and attract long-term investors with ESG preferences, ultimately lowering the firm’s cost of capital.
Eccles et al. (2014) conducted an in-depth investigation into this question, leveraging nearly 30 environmental and social policies categorized in the Thomson Reuters ASSET4 database (now LSEG). By calculating the proportion of sustainability policies adopted by each company relative to the total available policies, they identified 90 “high-sustainability companies” with an average adoption rate exceeding 40% from 1993 to 2009. Using Propensity Score Matching, the authors paired these 90 firms with “low-sustainability companies” that shared similar characteristics—such as industry, firm size, profitability, market-to-book ratio, and leverage—but had lower sustainability policy adoption rates. This approach enabled a comparison of differences in governance structures, strategies, operations, and financial performance between the two groups.
The findings revealed that high-sustainability companies are far more likely to assign sustainability responsibilities to their board of directors and establish a dedicated board committee for sustainability. They also tend to link executive compensation to environmental and social performance metrics. These firms typically implement more comprehensive and engaged stakeholder management processes, attract a higher proportion of long-term-oriented investors, and emphasize long-term information during conference calls with sell-side analysts. Compared to their low-sustainability counterparts, high-sustainability companies are more proactive in measuring information related to key stakeholders—such as employees, customers, and suppliers—and enhancing the credibility of these metrics through auditing procedures. Moreover, they not only collect but also disclose a greater volume of nonfinancial data. In terms of profitability, high-sustainability companies outperform their low-sustainability counterparts in both stock market and accounting performance. Using the Fama-French four-factor model to measure alpha (the stock’s abnormal return), the author found that high-sustainability companies generate significantly higher alpha than low-sustainability firms. Similarly, in accounting metrics, the former exhibit notably higher Return on Equity (ROE) and Return on Assets (ROA) compared to the latter. This superior performance is particularly pronounced in business-to-consumer (B2C) companies. The author attributes these results to the stronger brand reputation and more efficient use of natural resources by high-sustainability firms, reinforcing the impact mechanisms discussed earlier.
The positive impact of sustainability on corporate profitability, as discussed earlier, is commonly observed in empirical studies focusing on developed countries. However, do these findings hold when we consider global firms as a whole or focus specifically on developing countries with lower socioeconomic levels? Yu et al. (2018) addressed this question using data from 1,996 companies in the MSCI All Country World Index from 2012 to 2016, employing the Bloomberg ESG disclosure score (ranging from 0 to 100) to measure corporate sustainability. The study found that, overall, higher ESG scores significantly boost a firm’s Tobin’s Q, but the relationship is not simply linear. For companies with ESG scores below 10.38, marginal increases in scores actually reduce Tobin’s Q, whereas scores above this threshold enhance market value. Given that the average disclosure score for MSCI World Index companies is 33, improving sustainability practices holds practical value. 
What explains these discrepancies? Buchanan et al. (2018)’s perspective may offer valuable explanations: They argues that the impact of corporate sustainability on profitability and market value hinges on the relative dominance of two competing mechanisms. The first mechanism, which promotes profitability, has been discussed earlier: investments in sustainability strengthen relationships with stakeholders, balancing their diverse needs—such as consumers’ demand for eco-friendly products, employees’ expectations for better welfare, and investors’ requirements for governance transparency and long-term profitability. This reduces agency conflict costs and enhances corporate reputation, ultimately boosting profitability and long-term value.
Cultural and societal values add another layer of complexity. For instance, religious traditions or social norms may influence whether companies choose to disclose their social responsibility activities. In countries where resources are constrained and public awareness of ESG issues remains limited, should firms scale back ESG investments to prioritize core business operations? Or might such a short-term focus undermine their long-term competitiveness in global markets?
Current consumer demand trends further prompt us to ask: Why do Asia-Pacific consumers tend to prioritize health over low price, while European and American consumers often place stronger emphasis on cost-efficiency? This divergence suggests that firms must adopt differentiated product and marketing strategies tailored to local sustainability preferences. For example, in the Asia-Pacific market, companies may find greater success by highlighting nutritional value, wellness benefits, and product safety, whereas in Western markets, strategies emphasizing affordability and accessibility of sustainable products may resonate more strongly.
Taken together, NaiSnow’s initiatives have played a catalytic role in steering the tea beverage sector toward healthier and greener business models. Yet despite these achievements, several challenges remain. The company has not disclosed its annual carbon emissions or set clear emission-reduction targets, leading to gaps in environmental transparency. Recurrent food safety issues have also affected its public image, while long-standing profitability pressures expose weaknesses in its financial management. These factors collectively pose obstacles to the company’s long-term sustainability and highlight the complex trade-offs between brand innovation, operational execution, and corporate accountability.
From the investor’s perspective, whether these strategic shifts are fully “priced in” is also an important and practical issue. On one hand, as sustainable development gains traction in capital markets, investors are paying increasing attention to corporate social responsibility and long-term value creation. Companies that emphasize health and sustainability may benefit from stronger brand premiums, more stable market shares, and easier access to financing. On the other hand, if their sustainability strategies lack substance or financial backing, investors may view them merely as short-term marketing tools rather than genuine sources of long-term competitiveness.
Let’s examine the specific practices of some countries for a clearer understanding. For example, starting in 2025, the Canadian government requires that 100% of new light-duty fleet vehicle purchases be zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), with the goal of achieving a 100% ZEV light-duty fleet by 2030. This is supported by a series of green procurement initiatives, including clean technologies, environmental products, and services, to drive the transition to a net-zero and circular economy. The Italian government has developed a set of Minimum Environmental Criteria as the core foundation of its green procurement policy. By setting mandatory environmental requirements for various stages of the procurement process—including supplier selection, technical specifications, contract terms, and evaluation criteria—this framework helps government agencies identify the most environmentally friendly solutions. These Minimum Environmental Criteria apply to all public contracts, regardless of contract value, covering all government levels from central to local. This stringent mandatory policy not only effectively reduces the environmental impact of government procurement but also promotes the development of sustainable “circular” production and consumption systems. To date, these environmental criteria cover the following 20 procurement categories.
Investments in sustainability not only impact a company’s profitability but also reduce its operating risks, helping to prevent financial distress, delisting, or bankruptcy. In terms of earnings risk or operating risk, Arif et al. (2024) use the level of analysts' earnings forecast dispersion as a measure of future earnings risk, and find that ESG disclosures have a significant negative impact on a firm's future earnings risk. Additionally, disaggregated analysis of ESG disclosures shows that the impact of the social dimension is economically more pronounced than the environmental and governance disclosures, indicating that the disclosure of social issues carries more value than the other dimensions of ESG disclosures in minimising the dispersion of future earnings risk for a firm. This highlight that transparent information environment could serve as a tool to minimise the agency problem arising from the information asymmetry between corporate managers and shareholders concerning firms' future earnings. examined US listed companies, assessing the impact of ESG disclosures on future risk from both accounting-based and market-based perspectives. The study measured accounting-based earnings risk using the volatility of Return on Assets (ROA), defined as the standard deviation of ROA over five-year overlapping windows. For market-based risk, it used the overall volatility of stock returns (calculated as the standard deviation of monthly stock returns) and the idiosyncratic volatility of stock returns (derived as the annualized standard deviation of residuals from the Fama-French three-factor model). The findings confirmed that ESG disclosures significantly reduce a company’s operating risk.
Internal and External Trust Mechanism: High levels of ESG disclosure enhance a company’s social capital, such as customer loyalty, and foster enforceable mutual trust among management, shareholders, and other stakeholders. This collective trust helps curb excessive risk-taking, lowers the cost of equity capital, and encourages shareholders to support low-risk projects that ensure sustainable development(Godfrey, 2005). Meanwhile, management benefits from more stable career prospects by avoiding high-risk initiatives(Hoi et al., 2019).
EY identified nearly 400 trailblazers in the sample with mature sustainable supply chain management approaches and asked whether they had realized benefits from implementing these practices. Among them, 49% of trailblazers reported improved employee quality of life, while nearly 40% noted increased customer loyalty and lower employee turnover rates due to sustainable supply chain practices. The vast majority of companies expect that sustainable supply chain practices will enhance efficiency and productivity in the short term (1-3 years) or long term (3-5 years), thereby increasing revenue and improving profitability.
The researchers first explored the size factor, measuring it by market capitalization and then taking the natural logarithm of this metric. They performed a Pearson correlation regression on the companies’ ESG score data (ranging from 0 to 5). The results revealed a significant positive correlation between company size and ESG scores, with an R-squared value of 0.15, indicating that 15% of the variation in ESG scores among companies can be explained by size. This finding aligns with academic research by Baldini et al. (2018), whose empirical results showed that company size significantly and positively impacts Bloomberg ESG disclosure scores. The authors explained that larger firms face greater public scrutiny and pressure, leading to higher ESG disclosure to manage societal expectations, maintain legitimacy, and uphold a socially acceptable image.
In addition to the factors mentioned above, Tufano et al. (2024) argue that a company’s ownership structure significantly influences its sustainability performance. By analyzing 26,481 firm-year observations from 3,083 listed companies across 62 countries between 2002 and 2019, the study found that companies with founding families or other individual investors as material owners (i.e., where founding family members or individual investors are among the top ten shareholders) perform worse in ESG compared to firms with only institutional owners (which serve as the baseline category). This suggests that family or individual investors may prioritize short-term financial returns over long-term ESG goals. However, when family members serve as CEOs, the company’s ESG performance is significantly better than that of other firms, with an even stronger positive effect when descendants serve as CEOs (the coefficient for descendant CEOs is approximately double that of founding CEOs). This indicates that family involvement in management roles may drive longer-term, stakeholder-focused strategies, and generational succession in family firms may foster a stronger ESG orientation. Companies with non-family managers or government entities as major shareholders generally perform better in ESG, likely because government owners are driven by public policy or societal expectations to support environmental and social goals, while non-family managers may use ESG investments to reduce conflicts with stakeholders, thereby enhancing corporate reputation and operational stability. Companies with other listed firms as major shareholders tend to have poorer ESG performance, as these shareholder firms may prioritize short-term financial synergies or market competition over sustainable goals.
Investments are motivated by a positive intention, but this intention is not necessarily at the core of the investment strategy. Investors may seek to align their personal values with their investments but without a strong commitment to impact
Why does impact investing remain much smaller in scale compared with ESG integration or negative screening? Do you think impact investing could ever become mainstream, or will it remain a niche area reserved for a small pool of “altruistic capital”? Finally, compared with philanthropic giving, what is the greatest value of impact investing—its ability to combine measurable social and environmental outcomes with financial discipline?
This raises a critical question worth exploring: which driver—earnings growth, dividend yield, or P/E expansion—plays the dominant role in driving the returns of high ESG stock portfolios? Moreover, does the contribution of these three drivers vary in the short term versus the long term? To address the first question, Giese and Shah (2024) divided the earnings per unit of market capitalization (earnings/price) of a high ESG-rated stock portfolio (Q5) by that of a low ESG-rated portfolio (Q1). If this ratio increases over time, it indicates that the earnings of the high ESG portfolio are growing faster than those of the low ESG portfolio, suggesting that returns are driven by earnings growth. Conversely, a declining ratio would indicate the presence of P/E expansion. The findings, as shown in the figures below, reveal that while the Q5/Q1 ratio fluctuated over the decade, it generally trended upward. This suggests that, in the long term, earnings growth indeed plays a dominant role in boosting stock portfolio returns. Similarly, the researchers compared the dividend yields of Q5 and Q1, which also showed an upward trend, indicating that the contribution of dividend yield to returns is, in the long term, greater than that of P/E expansion.
For the second question, Gupta. et al. (2016) decomposed the contributions of these three factors to stock returns across different time horizons, as shown in the figure. In the short term (1-year period), P/E expansion contributed 51.1%, earnings growth 37.5%, and dividend yield only 11.4%. However, from the 1-year to the 20-year period, the contribution of P/E expansion to stock returns steadily declined. By the 3-year mark, returns driven by improvements in company fundamentals surpassed those driven by market sentiment. Over the 20-year long-term investment horizon, the vast majority of investment returns were driven by company fundamentals, with P/E expansion contributing less than 7%. In summary, the distribution of the three drivers’ contributions to returns over the long term aligns with our expectations. However, due to the dominant role of valuation adjustments in the short term, investors with shorter holding periods must carefully assess whether ESG portfolios carry bubble risks. 
The economic dimension of these improvements is equally significant. Zhang et al. (2019) estimated the costs and benefits of the Action Plan during its first five years (2013–2018). Nationwide expenditures on air pollution control steadily increased, amounting to a total of 1,651 billion yuan, of which Beijing contributed approximately 46.9 billion yuan. At the same time, improved air quality generated extensive benefits, including reduced public health risks, higher agricultural yields, slower material degradation, and lower cleaning costs. These benefits were valued at 2,469 billion yuan nationwide. As a result, the net benefit reached 818 billion yuan, with Beijing alone achieving net benefits exceeding 10 billion yuan. 
From a cost–benefit perspective, Beijing’s air quality campaign offers an important policy lesson. While the expenditures on pollution control were substantial, the overall societal benefits clearly outweighed the costs. This raises a broader question: how can policymakers effectively persuade the public and businesses to accept such high levels of upfront investment in environmental governance? One possible avenue lies in leveraging financial innovations such as green bonds or public–private partnerships (PPPs), which can mobilize private capital, distribute risks more efficiently, and enhance the long-term sustainability of pollution control initiatives.
Moreover, the cost–benefit evaluation framework used in Beijing provides a useful analytical tool that may extend beyond air pollution management. Specifically, it prompts further reflection on whether similar approaches could be applied to climate mitigation and adaptation policies, where the costs of resilience-building today may generate substantial long-term benefits in avoided damages, enhanced productivity, and improved public health.
This sequence of disasters underscores how climate change amplifies South Asia’s vulnerability to erratic monsoons. Intensified and unpredictable rainfall not only threatens lives and livelihoods in the short term but also undermines long-term economic recovery and sustainable development. In turn, this mounting climate risk has fueled rising demand for disaster financing, affordable insurance solutions, and large-scale investments in resilience building.
Comparisons between projected global climate damages and the costs of achieving the Paris Agreement’s mitigation targets reveal a striking imbalance. By 2050, the median projected economic damages from climate change are estimated to be about six times higher than the median costs of mitigation. This underscores that the economic damages of inaction far exceed the costs of mitigation, even though the net benefits of mitigation appear delayed. This delay arises because differences in climate damages between alternative emissions pathways are not statistically distinguishable until after mid-century. Beyond 2049, however, the estimates begin to diverge significantly, clearly demonstrating the long-term economic advantages of mitigation.
This evidence makes clear that climate investment is not only environmentally necessary but also economically rational. Although the upfront costs of mitigation and adaptation may seem substantial, the long-term damages from inaction—spanning infrastructure destruction, productivity decline, and systemic financial instability—are likely to be far more severe. By curbing the scale of future losses, climate action ultimately frees resources that can be reallocated to other societal priorities such as health care, infrastructure development, and national security. The central policy challenge, therefore, is not whether climate change should be addressed, but how to design governance and financial mechanisms that translate today’s expenditures into sustainable economic and social benefits over the long run.

根据文档内容，我已整理出企业ESG策略及利益相关者权衡的总结：

---

## 企业ESG策略与利益相关者权衡总结

### 一、核心张力：价值 vs 价值观

文档揭示了可持续金融中一个根本性矛盾：**如何在创造传统财务价值（股东财富、可衡量的业绩）的同时，融入价值观（气候责任、社会公平、长期可持续性）**。

---

### 二、主要ESG策略

| 策略类型 | 具体做法 |
|---------|---------|
| **信息披露** | 采用GRI、ISSB、TCFD等框架披露ESG原始数据 |
| **ESG评级整合** | 利用MSCI、Sustainalytics等评级优化投资决策 |
| **可持续金融产品** | 发行绿色债券、可持续发展挂钩贷款、ESG基金 |
| **供应链转型** | 推动绿色供应链、净零排放目标 |
| **利益相关者治理** | 从"股东至上"转向"利益相关者责任" |
| **混合融资** | 结合公共资本、慈善资本与私人资本 |

---

### 三、利益相关者之间的权衡

#### 1. 股东 vs 其他利益相关者
| 冲突点 | 说明 |
|-------|------|
| 消费者需求（环保产品）| 可能增加成本、压缩利润 |
| 员工期望（福利待遇）| 提升人力成本 |
| 社区要求（社会责任）| 分散管理层注意力 |
| 债权人诉求（风险控制）| 限制激进扩张策略 |

**机制**：ESG投资加强与各利益相关者的关系，降低代理冲突成本，提升企业声誉。

---

#### 2. 短期 vs 长期

| 维度 | 短期代价 | 长期收益 |
|-----|---------|---------|
| **财务回报** | ESG投入可能侵蚀当期利润 | 增强长期盈利能力和市场价值 |
| **投资回报** | 短期可能跑输传统基金 | 长期由盈利增长和股息驱动回报 |
| **生存压力** | 经济下行期ESG支出挤占核心运营资源 | 提升企业韧性和市场存续能力 |

**案例**：疫情期间（2019-2022），ESG投入高的企业财务状况反而较弱——**短期生存与长期可持续目标之间的权衡**。

---

#### 3. 金融机构的权衡

**银行面临的困境**：
- 彻底撤资 vs 提供转型融资支持可信脱碳计划
- 满足绿色转型支持义务 vs 维持盈利能力和信贷风险管理
- 政策激励可能导致道德风险（过度依赖补贴而非真正转型）

**投资者面临的抉择**：
- 财务回报 vs 社会影响
- 合规导向披露 vs 战略导向披露

---

#### 4. 合规成本 vs 市场认可

跨国企业需在不同司法管辖区协调不同ESG披露要求，面临：
- **合规成本**：满足多重标准的资源投入
- **市场认可**：高质量披露吸引绿色融资、降低资本成本

---

#### 5. 发达经济体 vs 发展中经济体

| 冲突领域 | 发达国家立场 | 发展中国家立场 |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| 气候融资 | 承诺2035年每年3000亿美元 | 需求1.3万亿美元，差距巨大 |
| 脱碳路径 | 激进减排要求 | 经济发展优先 |
| 技术转让 | 知识产权保护 | 技术获取诉求 |

---

### 四、缓解与管理建议

1. **动态平衡**：ESG策略需与企业财务能力、行业特性、宏观经济环境相匹配
2. **区分时机**：短期由市场情绪驱动，长期由企业基本面驱动
3. **避免过度承诺**：设定可量化的可持续目标，避免"漂绿"指控
4. **双重要性视角**：同时关注财务重要性和影响重要性
5. **混合融资工具**：利用公共资本撬动私人资本，降低风险

---

**关键结论**：ESG不是在财务回报与社会影响之间做零和选择，而是通过**战略性平衡短期代价与长期收益**，实现韧性增强、创新驱动和信任构建。成功的企业能在满足多元利益相关者需求的同时，维持财务可持续性。

Reliable data is essential for evaluating sustainability strategies. The ESG ratings industry is led by firms such as ISS, MSCI, LSEG, S&P Global, Sustainalytics, and Moody’s. These organizations assess companies on ESG criteria, helping investors make informed decisions. New entrants (e.g., Covalence, OWL ESG, and Truvalue Labs) are using advanced technologies like artificial intelligence, big data, and natural language processing to improve analysis. Despite concerns about the consistency and transparency of ESG scores, ESG ratings have helped raise standards and improve transparency across industries.
Investors, asset managers, and financial products
Investors are at the heart of the ESG ecosystem, using sustainability information to guide investment and risk management. Asset owners include pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, insurance companies, endowments, family offices, and retail investors. Asset managers oversee funds for these investors, including mutual funds, hedge funds, private equity, and infrastructure funds. On the financing side, banks and issuers structure ESG products such as green bonds and assess ESG risks when issuing debt.
As ESG has become more prominent, concerns about greenwashing and inconsistent practices have grown. Regulators and supervisors now play a key role in shaping the ESG landscape. International bodies like the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS), the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS), the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO), and the Financial Stability Board (FSB) have recognized sustainable risk alongside traditional financial risks. Governments are also active: the European Union leads with comprehensive ESG regulations, the UK has integrated ESG into corporate governance, and exchanges like the Hong Kong Stock Exchange require ESG reporting for listed companies.

A company’s financing activities form the foundational step for undertaking various projects. The core objective of financing is to secure the necessary funds to achieve strategic and operational goals. Broadly, financing can be divided into two categories: equity financing and debt financing. Equity financing involves raising funds by selling company shares, allowing investors to become shareholders and share in the company’s profits and growth potential, though it dilutes existing shareholders’ ownership. For instance, in 2022, Chinese wind power company Sany Renewable Energy conducted an initial public offering, raising nearly 5.5 billion RMB to fund production line upgrades, turbine R&D, and wind power industrial park projects. Debt financing, on the other hand, involves raising funds through issuing bonds or borrowing from financial institutions, requiring the company to repay the principal and interest within an agreed timeframe. For example, in 2022, Danish turbine manufacturer Vestas secured a 475 million EUR green loan from the European Investment Bank to support R&D for large-megawatt turbines. The choice between these financing methods depends not only on a company’s financial health, market conditions, and industry characteristics but also on external factors such as macroeconomic policies, financial market volatility, and investor preferences. In recent years, as concepts like sustainability and climate change have gained prominence, regulators and financial systems worldwide have increasingly integrated companies’ ESG performance into regulatory and investment decisions. Consequently, a company’s sustainability performance has become a key factor influencing its cost of capital and capital structure. This section will delve into how ESG performance affects financing costs for companies across countries, exploring the underlying economic principles and market mechanisms driving this phenomenon.
Sustainability and cost of debt
Developed economies, particularly the European Union, have taken the lead in integrating sustainability into financial systems. The EU’s 2018 Sustainable Finance Action Plan highlighted that achieving the 2030 energy and climate goals—reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 40% compared to 1990 levels, ensuring at least 27% of final energy consumption from renewables, and achieving at least 30% energy savings—requires an annual investment of €180 billion. Extending to sectors like transport, water, and waste would necessitate €270 billion. To align such large-scale funding with EU sustainability objectives, the EU established the EU Taxonomy, a unified classification system for climate, environmental, and socially sustainable activities, defining what qualifies as sustainable and identifying investment areas with the greatest sustainability impac. Additionally, the EU’s Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR) mandates disclosures at both product and entity levels. At the product level, portfolios managed by banks and investment firms, UCITS funds (Undertakings for Collective Investment in Transferable Securities), AIFs (Alternative Investment Funds), insurance-based investment products, and pension products must disclose their sustainability impacts (e.g., the proportion of the portfolio aligned with the EU Taxonomy) and the impact of sustainability risks on product returns. At the entity level, financial firms must disclose their sustainability risk policies and the Principal Adverse Impacts of their lending or investment decisions (e.g., GHG intensity of investee companies or exposure to controversial weapons). Clearly, these EU regulations have made it imperative for financial institutions to incorporate sustainability factors into lending or investment decisions (in this section, specifically bond investments).
To investigate whether corporate sustainability practices genuinely impact financing costs and to understand the mechanisms behind such effects, academic research has produced substantial empirical evidence from both developed and developing economies. Using a sample of global companies from 2002 to 2021
Houston and Shan (2022) show that banks are more likely to grant loans to borrowers with ESG profiles similar to their own and positively influence the borrower’s subsequent ESG performance.  Conditional on obtaining new loan financing within a 2-year period centered on the end date of the original loan, they find that borrowers are less likely to renew loans with the same lead lender(s) if there was a negative ESG-related reputational shock, increasing financial costs.  Apergis et al. (2022) examined bond issuance and used corporate bond yield spreads as a proxy for financing costs, reaching comparable conclusions. Eliwa et al. (2021) provided a more detailed exploration of the theoretical mechanisms driving this impact. According to stakeholder theory, stewardship theory, and transformational leadership theory, strong ESG practices signal to creditors a company’s management efficiency and integrity, demonstrating its ability to allocate resources effectively to meet stakeholder concerns while maintaining profitability. This reduces the likelihood of decisions that prioritize shareholders at the expense of creditors, mitigating agency conflict risks. Additionally, robust ESG performance lowers the reputational risks creditors face, leading to reduced interest rate demands. Furthermore, ESG disclosure itself reduces information asymmetry between lenders and companies, thereby decreasing risks associated with investment uncertainty. 
--
At the same time, if ESG ratings significantly affect financing costs, the risk of greenwashing becomes more pressing. Companies may feel tempted to enhance disclosures or emphasize selective achievements primarily to reduce their cost of capital, without delivering genuine sustainability improvements. This poses a challenge for both regulators and investors: what mechanisms—such as standardized disclosures, independent audits, or stricter penalty structures—can help ensure that lower capital costs are tied to real ESG progress rather than cosmetic compliance?
Case: Policy-driven green credit from China’s banks
We can gain a more intuitive understanding from a more micro perspective, such as the policies and practices of banks in different countries regarding sustainable lending. From the earlier chart, we can see that Chinese companies exhibit some of the largest gaps in the APAC region between high and low ESG-rated firms, whether in terms of cost of capital, cost of debt, or cost of equity. To support the national climate strategy of achieving carbon peak by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060, the former China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission issued the Guidelines for Green Finance in the Banking and Insurance Industries in 2022. Impact investing is an investment strategy that aims to generate positive and measurable social and environmental impacts alongside financial returns. Unlike thematic investing, which focuses primarily on stocks and bonds of companies, impact investing is more about providing finance for specific projects. These projects can include microfinance, community investing, social venture funds, funds with social impact objectives, and green and social bonds. Because its goal is to create positive social and environmental impacts, measuring and reporting these non-financial impacts is a key element of impact investing. South Asia, home to nearly two billion people, is among the world’s most climate-sensitive regions. The annual summer monsoon (June–September) delivers vital rainfall from the Indian Ocean, sustaining agriculture and replenishing reservoirs and aquifers. On average, monsoons traditionally account for about 70% of the region’s annual water supply. However, global warming is increasingly disrupting these patterns. Because warmer air retains more moisture, monsoon rainfall has become both more intense and more erratic, heightening the risks of droughts, extreme precipitation, and flash flooding.
The human and economic toll of these changes has been profound. In 2022, Pakistan suffered one of its most devastating floods in history: at least 33 million people were affected, more than 1,700 lives were lost, and economic damages exceeded $30 billion, with an additional $16.3 billion. estimated for rehabilitation and reconstruction. More recently, in August 2025, heavy monsoon rains and flash floods killed at least 700 people, displaced thousands, and destroyed homes and crops, while further extreme weather events were anticipated.
This sequence of disasters underscores how climate change amplifies South Asia’s vulnerability to erratic monsoons. Intensified and unpredictable rainfall not only threatens lives and livelihoods in the short term but also undermines long-term economic recovery and sustainable development. In turn, this mounting climate risk has fueled rising demand for disaster financing, affordable insurance solutions, and large-scale investments in resilience building.
From a financing perspective, several pressing questions emerge. Why do floods inflict disproportionately greater damage on low-income countries? How should international climate finance mechanisms—such as the Loss and Damage Fund—be designed and allocated to ensure fairness and effectiveness? And, if one were in the position of a development bank official, what kinds of financial instruments could be crafted to support rapid post-disaster recovery while also laying the foundation for long-term resilience?
